Client 2: And I’m Linda. I just want to know we’re okay month to month, and with healthcare stuff. The details make my eyes glaze over, honestly.
Advisor: Totally fair. I’ll keep it plain-English and we’ll tie everything back to your spending and peace of mind. Let’s start with income—what’s coming in over a year for the household, before taxes?
Client 1: All-in, our household gross income is 117650 a year.
Advisor: Got it. And is that mostly one source, or a couple different streams?
Client 1: It’s basically a pension that hits monthly, but if you annualize it, it’s still 117650.

Client 2: Yes—David is Client 1, I’m Client 2.
Advisor: Perfect. Now on the outflow side—about how much are you spending per month, total household expenses? Ballpark is fine, then we’ll refine later.
Client 2: Roughly forty-five hundred a month. The total monthly expenses are 4500.
Advisor: Thanks. And do you have any monthly debt payments—mortgage, car loans, credit cards—anything like that that’s a fixed payment each month?
Client 1: No, monthly debt payments total is 0. No mortgage, no car note.

Client 2: And I’m Linda. I just want to know we’re okay month to month, and with healthcare stuff. The details make my eyes glaze over, honestly.
Advisor: Totally fair. I’ll keep it plain-English and we’ll tie everything back to your spending and peace of mind. Let’s start with income—what’s coming in over a year for the household, before taxes?
Client 1: All-in, our household gross income is 117650 a year.
Advisor: Got it. And is that mostly one source, or a couple different streams?
Client 1: It’s basically a pension that hits monthly, but if you annualize it, it’s still 117650.

Client 1: No, monthly debt payments total is 0. No mortgage, no car note.
Advisor: Great—so no loans outstanding. Let me jot that down—one sec. Okay. Next I want to understand the investable pool—excluding the house—about what do you have across accounts you can draw from or manage for retirement?
Client 1: Investable assets total are 626100.
Advisor: Okay. Let’s break that into the main buckets so I understand where it sits and whose name it’s in. Do you have a joint taxable investment account?
Client 1: Yes. Joint taxable account on an advisor platform—value is 391400.

Client 2: I do. There’s a private markets holding at a brokerage—value is 24250.
Advisor: Okay. And then the home—just so we capture it—what’s the primary residence worth, roughly?
Client 1: Primary residence value is 339300.
Advisor: Thanks. And just to be clear, that home value is separate from the investable assets number you gave me earlier.
Client 1: Right—626100 is investable, and 339300 is the house.

Client 2: And I’m Linda. I just want to know we’re okay month to month, and with healthcare stuff. The details make my eyes glaze over, honestly.
Advisor: Totally fair. I’ll keep it plain-English and we’ll tie everything back to your spending and peace of mind. Let’s start with income—what’s coming in over a year for the household, before taxes?
Client 1: All-in, our household gross income is 117650 a year.
Advisor: Got it. And is that mostly one source, or a couple different streams?
Client 1: It’s basically a pension that hits monthly, but if you annualize it, it’s still 117650.

Client 1: All-in, our household gross income is 117650 a year.
Advisor: Got it. And is that mostly one source, or a couple different streams?
Client 1: It’s basically a pension that hits monthly, but if you annualize it, it’s still 117650.

Advisor: Hi David, hi Linda—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into the weeds, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m David. We’re kind of at that point where we want a plan we can stick to—retirement is here, and the market swings make me a little jumpy.
Client 2: And I’m Linda. I just want to know we’re okay month to month, and with healthcare stuff. The details make my eyes glaze over, honestly.
Advisor: Totally fair. I’ll keep it plain-English and we’ll tie everything back to your spending and peace of mind. Let’s start with income—what’s coming in over a year for the household, before taxes?
Client 1: All-in, our household gross income is 117650 a year.

Advisor: Perfect. And just to confirm country—are we talking US for your primary residence and planning, or do you split time elsewhere?
Client 2: US. We don’t have anything overseas.
Advisor: Great. Last one—risk comfort. Earlier you both sounded like you want growth, but you don’t want big surprises. If you had to pick a simple label, would you say conservative, moderate, or aggressive?
Client 1: Moderate.
Advisor: Okay. And Linda, does “moderate” feel right to you too, or would you shade it a bit more cautious?

Client 2: Sounds good—plain English, please.
Advisor: Deal. And if either of you think of any debt we forgot—like a store card you sometimes carry—just flag it, but for now I’ll keep it as zeros.
Advisor: Okay—thanks, David, thanks, Linda. Before we call it for today, I want to make sure I’ve got the household basics right. Are you two married?
Client 2: Yes, we’re married.
Advisor: Got it. And you’re both based in Florida, right—state of residence?

Advisor: Okay. And just so I’m not mixing you two up—David, you’re Client 1 and Linda you’re Client 2, right?
Client 2: Yes—David is Client 1, I’m Client 2.
Advisor: Perfect. Now on the outflow side—about how much are you spending per month, total household expenses? Ballpark is fine, then we’ll refine later.
Client 2: Roughly forty-five hundred a month. The total monthly expenses are 4500.
Advisor: Thanks. And do you have any monthly debt payments—mortgage, car loans, credit cards—anything like that that’s a fixed payment each month?

Advisor: Got it. Linda, does that match what you’ve seen when you file, or does it bounce around a bit year to year?
Client 2: It mostly matches—32% is the bracket we talk about, even if the actual bill wiggles.
Advisor: Okay. And last admin-ish thing: the way we work together—my firm is an RIA, a registered investment advisor. Is that the advisory channel you’re expecting here, versus like a brokerage-only relationship?
Client 2: Yes—RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Great, thanks. And just so I’m not leaving anything hanging from earlier—income is the pension, assets are those investment accounts plus the home, liabilities are zero, and protection is that long-term care policy. Sound right?

Client 2: And I’m Linda. I just want to know we’re okay month to month, and with healthcare stuff. The details make my eyes glaze over, honestly.
Advisor: Totally fair. I’ll keep it plain-English and we’ll tie everything back to your spending and peace of mind. Let’s start with income—what’s coming in over a year for the household, before taxes?

Client 1: Yes, number of adults is 2.
Advisor: Perfect. Next one is taxes—when you think about your marginal bracket, where do you usually land these days?
Client 1: We’re in the 32%.
Advisor: Got it. Linda, does that match what you’ve seen when you file, or does it bounce around a bit year to year?
Client 2: It mostly matches—32% is the bracket we talk about, even if the actual bill wiggles.

Client 2: Roughly mid-forties, give or take.
Advisor: Totally fair. If you can, what’s the exact annual figure you want me to use?
Client 2: Use 44500.
Advisor: Great—thank you. And that’s separate from David’s side, which we already talked through earlier.
Client 1: Yeah, that sounds right.

Advisor: Actually—sorry, one more thing before we really wrap. Linda, I want to make sure I’m attributing income correctly between you and David.
Client 2: Okay.
Advisor: For you specifically, are you fully retired at this point, or still doing any part-time work?
Client 2: I’m retired—no work income coming in.
Advisor: Got it. And when you think about your “role” in the household for planning purposes—David’s the primary point person on the accounts, but you’re his spouse, right?

Advisor: For you specifically, are you fully retired at this point, or still doing any part-time work?
Client 2: I’m retired—no work income coming in.
Advisor: Got it. And when you think about your “role” in the household for planning purposes—David’s the primary point person on the accounts, but you’re his spouse, right?
Client 2: Yes—spouse/partner.
